summary: Joe Reis shares his extensive experience with public speaking at tech conferences, breaking down the different types of events (community vs. commercial, vendor vs. vendor-agnostic), the significant work involved in preparing talks and managing travel, and the importance of understanding your value as a speaker. He emphasizes that speaking requires substantial time investment, can be emotionally draining due to travel demands, and speakers should increasingly expect compensation as their reputation grows.
key_points: Events range from small community meetups to large commercial conferences, with important distinctions between community-first and profit-driven organizations
Creating and delivering talks requires weeks or months of preparation, plus significant travel logistics
Speakers should expect compensation as their name is used to sell tickets - at minimum travel and accommodations
International travel takes a heavy emotional toll, including jet lag and extended time away from family
Many conference speaker rosters include paid sponsor placements alongside independent speakers, blurring the lines of authenticity
money_quotes: Meetups are still where you'll get the best conversations with practitioners.
Community-first means putting the community first, not one's pocketbook.
Travel seems sexy, but it can make you an emotional mess.

You need to get a cut if they're getting a cut. Some events will gaslight you and say they don't pay speakers for some reason. Dig into this reason to understand if they're telling the truth or if they're full of shit. It's usually the latter.
I don't think most people should do it because the ROI isn't there.
key_insight: Public speaking at tech conferences requires significant preparation and travel that most people underestimate, and speakers should advocate for fair compensation once their name carries value, while being wary of events that 'community-wash' their profit motives.
